summary: Puritan general and statesman who commanded the Parliamentarian New Model Army in the English Civil War, signed Charles I's death warrant, brutally conquered Ireland, and ruled as Lord Protector of the Commonwealth of England, Scotland, and Ireland from 1653 until his death in 1658. Identified in giveaways as "Old Ironsides," the man who took over the New Model Army from Thomas Fairfax, and the subject of Andrew Marvell's "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland." His conquest of Ireland (Drogheda, Wexford) and his rule via the Instrument of Government and the Rule of the Major-Generals are his most-clued acts.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Cromwell is identified in giveaways: the Puritan general who rose through the New Model Army, taking effective control of it from its nominal commander Thomas Fairfax, and who subsequently ruled as Lord Protector of the Commonwealth of England, Scotland, and Ireland. His cavalry unit was nicknamed the "Ironsides," a name later extended to him personally.

clues.clue: Served as Lord Protector of England, Scotland, and Ireland
examples: for 10 points, what "lord protector" emerged victorious in the English Civil War?
name this Lord Protector of England.

clues.clue: Puritan general who took control of the New Model Army from Thomas Fairfax and became Lord Protector of the Commonwealth of England
examples: name this Puritan general of the New Model Army and Lord Protector of the Commonwealth of England.
name this man who took control of the New Model Army from Thomas Fairfax and later became Lord Protector of England.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Old Ironsides," a name derived from his cavalry unit's nickname
examples: "Old Ironsides" was the nickname of, for 10 points, what first Lord Protector of England?

clues.clue: Leader of the Roundhead faction during the English Civil War
examples: name this Lord Protector of England and leader of the Roundhead faction during the English Civil War.

works.name: English Civil War: Rise to Power
works.description: Cromwell rose from a cavalry commander (the "Ironsides") to second-in-command of the New Model Army under Thomas Fairfax, with whom he clashed, before becoming one of the regicides who sent Charles I to the block and won the war's climactic victories.

clues.clue: Helped organize and command the New Model Army to victory at the Battle of Naseby (1645) as second-in-command under Thomas Fairfax, with whom he later feuded
examples: The Battle of Naseby was won by this man's New Model Army.
This leader had disputes with his superior, Thomas Fairfax, under whom he helped win the Battle of Naseby.

clues.clue: Commanded the "Ironsides" cavalry unit as father-in-law of Henry Ireton
examples: This father-in-law of Henry Ireton commanded the "Ironsides" cavalry unit.

clues.clue: Won the last battle of the (First) English Civil War at Preston (1648)
examples: The Drogheda massacre was carried out by forces under the command of this future Lord Protector, who had previously won the last victory of the English civil war in 1648 at Preston.

clues.clue: Conducted the Siege of Pembroke during the Second English Civil War, suppressing the Royalist revolt in Wales
examples: The Siege of Pembroke was conducted by this Parliamentarian leader, who would later become Lord Protector of the Commonwealth of England, Scotland, and Ireland.

clues.clue: Was among the regicides who signed Charles I's death warrant (traditionally counted the third signatory) and advocated for the king's execution
examples: This man was the third person to sign Charles I's death warrant and had his corpse beheaded by Charles II.
One of Carlyle's examples of heroes in history is this English statesman who advocated for the execution of Charles I and served as Lord Protector of England after commanding the New Model Army.

clues.clue: Defeated Charles II and an invading Scottish army at the Battle of Worcester (1651), after which Charles evaded capture by hiding in an oak tree at Boscobel House
examples: He won a battle in which the losing commander evaded capture by hiding with William Careless in an oak tree at Boscobel House.
At the Norway Debate in 1940, Leo Amery quoted this victor of the Battle of Worcester when he told Neville Chamberlain "in the name of God, go!"

works.name: Conquest of Ireland
works.description: Cromwell's 1649-50 invasion of Ireland ended the Eleven Years' War (Irish Confederate Wars) but is remembered above all for the massacres at Drogheda and Wexford; the ensuing land settlement dispossessed Irish Catholics on a massive scale.

clues.clue: At the Siege of Drogheda, the defending commander Arthur Aston was legendarily beaten to death with his own wooden leg, which his attackers believed concealed gold coins (some accounts place this at Millmount Fort)
examples: An army under this leader killed Arthur Aston with his own wooden leg which they believed contained coins at Millmount Fort.
This leader passed the Penal Laws during a campaign in which he gave no quarter to the defeated garrison of Drogheda; that campaign was his invasion of Ireland.

clues.clue: At the Siege of Wexford, New Model Army troops sacked the town in 1649, shooting or drowning over 1,000 civilians in the River Slaney after Cromwell rejected town governor David Sinnot's attempt to negotiate surrender
examples: His forces killed David Sinnot, who was attempting to negotiate a surrender.

One atrocity committed by the New Model Army during Cromwell's invasion of Ireland was the sacking of this town in 1649. Over 1,000 civilians were shot or drowned in the River Slaney here after Cromwell rejected David Sinnot's attempts to negotiate.

clues.clue: Victory at the Battle of Rathmines (1649) allowed his troops, including those under son-in-law Henry Ireton, to land in Ireland and begin the campaign
examples: Victory at Rathmines allowed troops to carry out a campaign under this commander with support from Henry Ireton.
The Battle of Rathmines was the first battle in this campaign, which featured a siege at Wexford.

clues.clue: The conquest brought the Eleven Years' War (Irish Confederate Wars) to an end
examples: This person led a campaign that ended the Eleven Years' War.
The Eleven Years' War ended as a result of this campaign.

clues.clue: Passed the Penal Laws to force Irish Catholics to convert to Protestantism, and had troops under John Hewson and Hardress Waller systematically destroy Irish food supplies
examples: After this event, the Penal Laws were passed to force Catholics to convert to Protestantism.
Name this event that was followed by the passage of several Penal Laws and saw John Hewson and Hardress Waller systematically destroy the food supplies of one side.

clues.clue: The Rump Parliament's Act of Settlement confiscated land and forced surviving Irish Catholic landowners to relocate to Connacht ("to Hell or Connacht"), while the earlier Adventurers' Act rewarded government investors with confiscated land and poor colonists nicknamed "Redlegs" were deported to Barbados
examples: An Act of Settlement passed during this man's term by the Rump Parliament forced natives to Connaught.
The term "Redlegs" referred to poor people deported to Barbados during a campaign named for this man.

clues.clue: The land settlement with the Irish heavily favored Protestant settlers in Ulster, many of whom had been massacred during the 1641 rebellion
examples: Cromwell's settlement with the Irish heavily favored the Protestant settlers in this northern province, many of whom had been massacred during the rebellion of 1641.

clues.clue: Ordered one of the earliest national surveys, the Down Survey, to map confiscated Irish land
examples: This leader ordered one of the earliest national surveys, the Down Survey.

clues.clue: During the campaign, a setback occurred at the city of Clonmel
examples: One setback during this campaign came at the city of Clonmel.

clues.clue: Crushed the Irish Catholic Confederation
examples: This person crushed the Irish Catholic Confederation and established Barebone's Parliament.

works.name: Lord Protector: Government of the Protectorate
works.description: As Lord Protector, Cromwell governed under England's only written constitution, purged and dissolved successive Parliaments, and briefly imposed direct military rule through the Major-Generals before a more conventional settlement restored some civilian government.

clues.clue: Governed under the Instrument of Government (1653), England's only written constitution, largely based on the Grandees' "Heads of Proposals" and restricting the franchise to those owning over 200 pounds of property
examples: This man's initial charter of government restricted the franchise to those with more than 200 pounds' worth of property; that document was the Instrument of Government.
This ruler...was given his highest position by a document drafted by James Lambert and based partly on the "Heads of Proposals".

clues.clue: Forcibly dissolved the Rump Parliament, dismissing it by pointing at the ceremonial mace and declaring "take away that fool's bauble," then set up the nominated Barebones Parliament (1653)
examples: In a speech dismissing an assembly, this man indicated a ceremonial mace and commanded "take away that fool's bauble."
He carried out a purge of the Rump Parliament to create the Barebones Parliament.

clues.clue: Instituted the Rule of the Major-Generals (1655-57), dividing England into twelve military districts policed by militias funded by a "decimation tax" levied on former Royalists
examples: This leader divided his country into twelve regions, each of which was policed by a militia funded by the "decimation tax."
This man joined John Dalbier at the siege of Basing House shortly after leading a victory at Devizes Castle and began the Rule of the Major-Generals after a failed invasion of Hispaniola.

clues.clue: Ended the Rule of the Major-Generals after debate over the Humble Petition and Advice (1657), which overruled the Instrument of Government and created the "Other House" (partly to address the uproar over Quaker James Naylor's messianic re-enactment of Christ's entry into Jerusalem)
examples: After three months of debate over the Humble Petition and Advice, this man ended the Rule of the Major Generals and overruled the Instrument of Government.
This leader created the "Other House" to deal with the uproar over James Naylor's imitation of Christ's entry into Jerusalem.

clues.clue: Convened the Whitehall Conference to debate whether to readmit Jews to England, a measure he personally favored
examples: This leader convened the Whitehall Conference to debate whether England should readmit the Jews, which he favored.

clues.clue: Militias under his rule attempted to suppress bear-baiting, stage plays, and the celebration of Christmas
examples: Under this ruler, militias attempted to abolish bear-baiting, stage plays, and Christmas.

works.name: Suppression of Radicals: Levellers, Mutinies & Plots
works.description: Cromwell repeatedly clashed with more radical factions to his left, from the Leveller movement's constitutional demands at Putney to army mutinies and religious radicals, and survived at least one serious assassination plot uncovered by his intelligence network.

clues.clue: Chaired the Putney Debates (1647) against the Levellers, led in the debate by Colonel Thomas Rainsborough, whose "Agreement of the People" was considered alongside "The Case of the Army Stated"; supported by son-in-law Henry Ireton, Cromwell questioned whether England would be made "like Switzerland"
examples: At the Putney Debates, when the Levellers debated the future of English democracy against less radical members of the New Model Army, this man chaired the proceedings. His son-in-law, Henry Ireton, argued against the Levellers.
This man asked if his country would be made "Like Switzerland" and his principal supporter repeatedly asked if an unjust or dangerous agreement need be kept.

clues.clue: Suppressed the Corkbush Field (Ware) Mutiny after Leveller-sympathizing troops refused to pledge loyalty to Thomas Fairfax; after the later Banbury Mutiny (prompted by a regiment's refusal to serve in Ireland over the violated Solemn Engagement), he executed three Leveller soldiers at Burford
examples: This leader suppressed the Corkbush Field mutiny after troops refused to declare loyalty to his commander.
After the Banbury Mutiny, this person executed three members of the Leveller movement.

clues.clue: Quelled the Bishopsgate mutiny, which led to the execution of Robert Lockyer
examples: This person quelled the Bishopsgate mutiny, which led to the execution of Robert Lockyer.

clues.clue: Was criticized as "the dissemblingest perjured villain in the world" by Fifth Monarchist preachers after being named Lord Protector in 1653, despite his initial sympathy for their millenarian cause
examples: Despite his initial sympathy with their cause, this politician was criticised as "the dissemblingest perjured villain in the world" by Fifth Monarchist preachers after he was named Lord Protector in 1653.

clues.clue: Smashed the Royalist "Sealed Knot" conspiracy using intelligence gathered by spymaster John Thurloe
examples: This leader smashed the Sealed Knot cabal with intelligence gathered by the genius spymaster John Thurloe.

clues.clue: Was the target of the anonymous 1657 pamphlet Killing No Murder, which advocated his assassination and was written after Miles Sindercombe's failed plot to burn Whitehall Palace and kill him
examples: The pamphlet was addressed to this Lord Protector, advocating his assassination.
This ruler, whose death was called for in the pamphlet "Killing no Murder", was given his highest position by a document drafted by James Lambert.

works.name: Foreign & Colonial Policy
works.description: Cromwell's foreign policy pursued Protestant and commercial interests abroad: an alliance with France that netted Dunkirk, an ambitious but only partly successful campaign against Spain's American empire, and expeditions to secure or settle England's Atlantic colonies.

clues.clue: Commissioned William Penn and Robert Venables to lead the "Western Design" against the Spanish West Indies, which failed to capture Hispaniola but did seize Jamaica
examples: This leader commissioned William Penn and Robert Venables to attack the Spanish West Indies in the "Western Design," which failed to capture Hispaniola but did take Jamaica.
Robert Venables, who had served this leader at the Battle of Rathmines, led his campaign to conquer the Caribbean called the "Western Design."

clues.clue: After Robert Blake invaded the pro-Royalist Isles of Scilly and destroyed King Charles's Castle, a new fortification was built and named Cromwell's Castle in his honor
examples: After invading the pro-Royalist Isles of Scilly, Robert Blake blew up King Charles's Castle and used some of the remaining stone to build another castle, which was named for this Lord Protector of England.

clues.clue: An alliance with France resulted in England gaining the port of Dunkirk
examples: He created a system of military rule by major generals, and his French alliance ended up giving his country Dunkirk.

clues.clue: William Sayle left his post as Governor of Bermuda to lead an expedition to Preacher's Cave in support of Cromwell's government
examples: In support of this leader, William Sayle left his post as Governor of Bermuda to lead an expedition to Preacher's Cave.

works.name: Death, Succession & Posthumous Execution
works.description: Cromwell died in 1658 and was succeeded by his ineffectual son Richard, whose brief tenure collapsed into the Restoration; the returning royalist regime took its revenge on Cromwell's corpse itself.

clues.clue: Was succeeded as Lord Protector by his son Richard Cromwell, whose incompetence led Parliament to restore Charles II
examples: His son was so incompetent that Parliament voted to restore Charles II.

clues.clue: After the Restoration, his corpse was disinterred and posthumously "executed"/beheaded; his head was later displayed on a pike before eventually reaching Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, where a 1934 study by Karl Pearson and Geoffrey Morant argued for its authenticity
examples: This man was the third person to sign Charles I's death warrant and had his corpse beheaded by Charles II.
Name this object now residing at Sidney Sussex College, the subject of an exhaustive 1934 study by Karl Pearson and Geoffrey Morant that claimed to confirm its authenticity.

works.name: Cultural Depictions: Andrew Marvell's Cromwell Odes
works.description: Andrew Marvell wrote two major Horatian odes to Cromwell that dominate his literary afterlife: "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" (1650), a poem of guarded celebration written just after the Irish campaign, and the later "The First Anniversary" (1655), praising his rule as Lord Protector.

clues.clue: In "An Horatian Ode," Cromwell is the "restless" figure who "could not cease / In the inglorious arts of peace," unable to remain idle even after his military triumphs
examples: This "restless" person "could not cease / In the inglorious arts of peace" in that "Horatian Ode" by Andrew Marvell.
Name this "restless" man who could not cease "in the inglorious arts of peace" in a poem written in the style of Horace.

clues.clue: The poem compares him to "three-fork'd lightning, first / breaking the clouds where it was nurst" before blasting through the laurels on "Caesar's head"
examples: This person is compared to "three-fork'd lightning" in a poem that, despite its title, takes a Pindaric public subject.
Eliot notes the "proper degree of seriousness" of Marvell's wit in treating subjects such as this person, whom Marvell compared to "three-fork'd lightning."

clues.clue: The poem says "The Pict no shelter now shall find" within his "parti-colour'd mind," comparing him to Caesar and Hannibal in their relation to Gaul and Italy
examples: This poem says that "The Pict no shelter now shall find" within the "parti-colour'd mind" of its subject, who will prove to be as Caesar and Hannibal were to Gaul and Italy.
Name this subject of an ode which asks, 'What may not then our isle presume/While victory his crest does plume' now that 'The Pict no shelter now shall find / Within his parti-colour'd mind.'

clues.clue: Marvell's ode criticizes another figure for living "reserved and austere" in his "private gardens" as if his "highest plot" were "to plant the bergamot," contrasted with Cromwell's restlessness
examples: In the poem, Cromwell is praised as a man, who from his "private gardens, where he lived reserved and austere" spent his time, as if his "highest plot" was to plant this kind of flower.
A person in this poem weaves a metaphorical net by "twining subtle fears with hope", while another person is criticized for living "reserved and austere".

clues.clue: Called "the War's and Fortune's son" and urged to "march indefatigably on" in the poem's penultimate stanza
examples: Its penultimate stanza urges the title figure, "the War's and Fortune's son," to "march indefatigably on".

clues.clue: In "The First Anniversary," Marvell says he "contracted" the "force of scattered time" and was "chosen out of better Earth" than other kings
examples: Name this man, who in the poem "The First Anniversary" is said to have "contracted" the "force of scattered time" and was "chosen out of better Earth" than other kings.

works.name: Cultural Depictions: Other Literary & Historiographical Treatments
works.description: Beyond Marvell, Cromwell has been a recurring subject for English poets and historians, from contemporaries who praised or damned him to later writers who used him as a touchstone for arguments about heroism and historical memory.

clues.clue: Subject of John Milton's Sonnet XVI ("To the Lord General Cromwell, May 1652") and Edmund Waller's earliest "Panegyrick"
examples: Name this subject of Edmund Waller's earliest "Panegyrick" and Milton's sixteenth sonnet.

clues.clue: John Dryden praised the Protectorate in his "Heroic Stanzas on the Death of Oliver Cromwell," then reversed course to praise Charles II in "Astraea Redux"
examples: Despite praising the Protectorate in his "Heroic Stanzas on the Death of Oliver Cromwell," John Dryden changed his stance and praised Charles II in this other poem, which calls the king a "happy prince."

clues.clue: Thomas Gray's "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard" imagines a village dead who might have been "some Cromwell guiltless of his country's blood," resting beside a "mute inglorious Milton"
examples: This Thomas Gray poem mentions "Some Cromwell guiltless of his country's blood," resting alongside a "mute inglorious Milton."
After noting that "Some mute inglorious Milton here may rest," Gray writes that someone like this leader, "guiltless of his country's blood," might rest in the churchyard.

clues.clue: Thomas Carlyle cited him as an example of a hero in history in On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, part of Carlyle's "great man theory"
examples: One of Carlyle's examples of heroes in history is this English statesman who advocated for the execution of Charles I and served as Lord Protector of England after commanding the New Model Army.

clues.clue: T. S. Eliot's essay "Andrew Marvell" cites Marvell's treatment of Cromwell as an example of the poet's "proper degree of seriousness"
examples: Eliot notes the "proper degree of seriousness" of Marvell's wit in treating subjects such as this person, whom Marvell compared to "three-fork'd lightning."

clues.clue: Is the title figure of Victor Hugo's massive verse play Cromwell, whose preface served as a manifesto for Shakespearean-style Romantic French drama
examples: The preface to Victor Hugo's massive verse play about this person served as a manifesto for a Shakespearean-style Romantic French drama.

comprehensive_summary: Oliver Cromwell (1599-1658) was a Puritan gentleman-turned-soldier who became the dominant figure of the English Civil Wars and the Interregnum that followed them. He rose through the Parliamentarian cause commanding the "Ironsides" cavalry, then served as second-in-command of the New Model Army under Thomas Fairfax, with whom he clashed, helping win the decisive Battle of Naseby (1645) and, at the close of the Second Civil War, the last battle of that conflict at Preston (1648) and the Siege of Pembroke in Wales. He was among the regicides who signed Charles I's death warrant (traditionally counted the third signatory) and advocated for the king's execution. In 1649-50 he led the brutal conquest of Ireland, ending the Eleven Years' War: his troops massacred the garrison and townspeople at the Siege of Drogheda (where commander Arthur Aston was legendarily beaten to death with his own wooden leg) and at the Siege of Wexford, after landing via the Battle of Rathmines with son-in-law Henry Ireton. The conquest was followed by the Penal Laws and a punitive land settlement: the Act of Settlement drove dispossessed Irish Catholics to Connacht ("to Hell or Connacht"), the Adventurers' Act rewarded government investors with confiscated land, poor colonists ("Redlegs") were deported to Barbados, and Ulster was resettled with Protestants. Recalled to England, he crushed a Scottish invasion supporting Charles II at the Battle of Worcester (1651), after which Charles famously hid in an oak tree at Boscobel House. As Lord Protector from 1653, Cromwell governed under the Instrument of Government, England's only written constitution, having dissolved the Rump Parliament ("take away that fool's bauble") and set up the nominated Barebones Parliament. He instituted the Rule of the Major-Generals, dividing England into twelve militarily policed districts funded by a "decimation tax," before ending that regime after the Humble Petition and Advice created the "Other House." He repeatedly suppressed radicals to his political left: chairing the Putney Debates against the Levellers (opposed there by Thomas Rainsborough, supported by Henry Ireton), crushing the Corkbush Field and Banbury mutinies, quelling the Bishopsgate mutiny, and drawing criticism from the millenarian Fifth Monarchists. He survived the Sealed Knot conspiracy (foiled by spymaster John Thurloe) and Miles Sindercombe's assassination plot, which prompted the pamphlet Killing No Murder. Abroad, he pursued the "Western Design" against Spain's Caribbean colonies (gaining Jamaica but failing to take Hispaniola), allied with France to gain Dunkirk, and secured England's Atlantic colonies, including the Isles of Scilly (later home to Cromwell's Castle) and Bermuda. He died in 1658 and was succeeded by his son Richard Cromwell, whose weak rule collapsed into the Restoration; after Charles II's return, Cromwell's corpse was disinterred and posthumously "executed," his head eventually reaching Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge. He became a fixture of English literature and historiography: Andrew Marvell's "An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland" and "The First Anniversary" are his most-quoted treatments ("could not cease / In the inglorious arts of peace," compared to "three-fork'd lightning"), alongside poems by Milton and Waller, John Dryden's "Heroic Stanzas on the Death of Oliver Cromwell," Thomas Gray's line about "some Cromwell guiltless of his country's blood" in the "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard," Thomas Carlyle's use of him as an exemplar of the "great man theory" of history, T. S. Eliot's essay on Marvell, and Victor Hugo's Romantic-manifesto verse play Cromwell.
recursive_suggestions: Thomas Cromwell — a distinct historical figure (Henry VIII's chief minister, protagonist of Hilary Mantel's Wolf Hall trilogy); several fetched questions were about him and were excluded here. Watch for confusion when generating cross-refs.
'An Horatian Ode upon Cromwell's Return from Ireland' — recurring standalone Literature answerline for Marvell's poem; could merit its own topic page
Andrew Marvell — author of both Cromwell odes; no page yet, high-value Literature topic
Henry Ireton — Cromwell's son-in-law and lieutenant in Ireland, has independent bonus-part presence (2x recurring answer)

Oliver Cromwell's conquest of Ireland — recurring standalone Events answerline (2 tossups fetched separately); currently folded into this page's 'Conquest of Ireland' section but could be split out given the volume of material
Battle of Naseby / Battle of Worcester — each recurs as an independent bonus-part answerline
Richard Cromwell — his son and successor as Lord Protector, has independent bonus-part presence
Tower of London and Finland — unrelated topics that surfaced in the raw search results (matched on incidental mentions of a 'Cromwell' or search noise) and were excluded entirely
